items.question: There's ___ milk in the fridge.
explanations: 'A milk' is wrong — milk is uncountable.

items.question: I don't have ___ money.
explanations: 'A money' is wrong — money is uncountable.

items.question: Would you like ___ tea?
explanations: Correct! Offers use 'some': 'Would you like some tea?'
'Any' would be unusual in a friendly offer.
'A tea' can mean a cup, but 'some tea' fits the offer best.

items.question: Are there ___ tickets left?
explanations: Correct! A normal question takes 'any': 'any tickets'.
'Some' is used in offers, not neutral questions.

items.question: Can I have ___ water, please?
explanations: Correct! Requests use 'some': 'Can I have some water?'

items.question: I saw ___ at the door but I'm not sure who.
We didn't buy ___ at the shop.
explanations: 'Didn't buy nothing' is a double negative.

items.question: You can sit in ___ seat — they're all free.
explanations: Correct! 'Any' here means 'it doesn't matter which'.

items.question: Correct this: 'Would you like any biscuits?'
explanations: Correct! Offers use 'some': 'Would you like some biscuits?'
'Many biscuits' changes the meaning to a large number.
'Any' is unusual in an offer expecting yes.

items.question: Which sentence type uses 'some'?
explanations: Most questions take 'any' (offers are the exception).
